questions.question: In RL, an agent learns by:
options: Memorising training labels
Interacting with an environment and receiving reward signals
questions.explanation: No labelled data. The agent discovers what works by trial and error.

questions.question: The reward signal in RL is:
options: Always immediate
Feedback (positive or negative) that tells the agent how good its action was -- can be delayed
questions.explanation: Delayed rewards (e.g. winning the game) make credit assignment hard.

questions.question: The exploration-exploitation trade-off means:
options: Finding the best exploration rate
Balancing trying new actions (exploration) vs. using known good actions (exploitation)
questions.explanation: Too much exploitation: gets stuck in local optima. Too much exploration: slow learning.

questions.question: A Markov Decision Process (MDP) formalises RL as:
options: A supervised problem
States, Actions, Transitions, Rewards, and Discount factor (gamma)
questions.explanation: RL problems are usually modelled as MDPs: current state fully determines transition probabilities.

questions.question: The discount factor gamma in RL:
options: Is always 1
Controls how much future rewards are worth relative to immediate rewards (0=greedy, 1=patient)
questions.explanation: gamma=0.99: agent values future rewards almost as much as immediate ones.

questions.question: Model-free RL learns:
options: From a model of the environment
Directly from interactions, without building an explicit model of the environment
questions.explanation: Q-learning, PPO are model-free. Model-based RL builds an explicit world model.

questions.question: The policy in RL maps:
options: States to values
States to actions (or action probability distributions)
questions.explanation: pi(a|s): given state s, what action a should I take?

questions.question: RL has been successfully applied to:
options: Only simple games
Game playing, recommendation systems, robotics, LLM alignment (RLHF), resource management
questions.explanation: AlphaGo, ChatGPT (RLHF), recommendation systems all use RL.

questions.question: Off-policy learning in RL:
options: Only updates on current policy actions
Can learn from data collected by a different (older) policy -- more sample efficient
questions.explanation: Q-learning is off-policy: learns optimal Q regardless of the exploration policy used.

questions.question: The biggest challenge in real-world RL compared to games:
options: More compute
Sparse rewards, sample inefficiency, safety constraints, and partial observability in real environments
questions.explanation: Games have dense rewards and perfect state information. Real world is much harder.
